description: Architectural patterns for building AI enemies with behavior trees, controllers, and perception in UE5

entries.title: Pattern: AI Enemy (Behavior Tree)
entries.description: How to structure an AI enemy using UE5's built-in AI framework: AIController for the brain, Behavior Tree for decisions, Blackboard for memory, and Perception for senses. Each piece has a clear role.
entries.whatItIs: The standard UE5 architecture for AI enemies. The enemy Character is just a body — the AIController is the brain that runs a Behavior Tree, reads sensory data from AI Perception, and stores knowledge in a Blackboard.
whenToUse: Any game with AI enemies that patrol, chase, attack, and react to the player
When enemies need different behaviors based on state (idle, alert, combat, fleeing)
When designers need to tune AI behavior without touching C++ code
whenNotToUse: Very simple AI that just walks toward the player — a basic Tick with MoveToActor is enough
Swarm/horde AI with hundreds of identical enemies — Behavior Trees per-agent is too expensive, use a central manager
State Tree is the newer alternative for simpler state-based AI — consider it for straightforward decision logic
keyPoints: STEP 1 — Create the enemy Character class (AEnemyCharacter). It handles: mesh, capsule, movement component, health component, animations. It does NOT make decisions — it's the body.
STEP 2 — Create a custom AIController (AEnemyAIController). Assign it to the character via AIControllerClass in the constructor or editor. The controller is the brain.
STEP 3 — Create a Blackboard Data Asset. Define keys for what the AI needs to remember: TargetActor (Object), PatrolLocation (Vector), AlertLevel (Float), HasLineOfSight (Bool).
STEP 4 — Create a Behavior Tree Asset. Structure it with a Selector at the root: Combat branch (has target + in range), Chase branch (has target + not in range), Patrol branch (fallback).
STEP 5 — Add UAIPerceptionComponent to the AIController. Configure senses: Sight (main detection), Hearing (react to noise), Damage (react to being hit). Bind OnPerceptionUpdated.
STEP 6 — When perception detects/loses the player, update Blackboard keys. The Behavior Tree reacts to key changes through its decorators — no manual state machine needed.
STEP 7 — Use BT Tasks for actions (MoveTo, Attack, Wait), Decorators for conditions (HasBlackboardValue, IsAtLocation), and Services for continuous updates (update distance to target).
STEP 8 — For combat: create BT Tasks that call functions on the Character (PlayAttackMontage, UseAbility). The BT decides WHEN to attack, the Character handles HOW.
commonMistakes: Putting AI decision logic in the Character class — the Character is the body, the AIController is the brain
Not using Blackboard — hardcoding state variables in C++ instead of using the data-driven Blackboard that the BT can query
Overcomplicating the first Behavior Tree — start with: See player? Chase. In range? Attack. Otherwise? Patrol. Add complexity later.
Forgetting to set AIControllerClass on the Character — without it, no controller spawns and the BT never runs
Running expensive logic (traces, pathfinding) every Tick instead of using BT Services on intervals
Not using AI Perception — manually checking distance/line of sight in Tick instead of letting the engine handle it
Making AI too dependent on the Player class — use the perception system and Blackboard so AI works with any target
goodPractices: Character = body (visuals, animations, health). AIController = brain (decisions, perception, BT). Keep them separate.
Blackboard is the AI's memory — everything the AI knows goes here. The BT reads from it, perception writes to it.
Start with a simple BT and add complexity through new branches. A working simple AI is better than a broken complex one.
Use EQS (Environment Query System) for spatial decisions: finding cover, choosing patrol points, selecting targets
Create reusable BT Tasks: BTTask_MoveToTarget, BTTask_PlayMontage, BTTask_Wait — share across enemy types
Different enemy types = different Behavior Trees on the same AIController base class. A ranged enemy and melee enemy share the controller but run different trees.
Use BT Services for periodic updates (every 0.5s) instead of Tick — cheaper and more predictable
